past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, well controlled on amlodipine
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with metformin
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, managed with tamsulosin
COVID-19 infection confirmed by RT-PCR approximately 2 weeks prior to presentation
family_history: Father: ischemic stroke at age 72, deceased
Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypothyroidism
Brother: coronary artery disease, stent placement at age 58
No family history of autoimmune disease or psychiatric illness

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 10 years ago (20 pack-year history)
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home; independent in all ADLs prior to this illness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but apathetic with flat affect. Oriented to person only; disoriented to place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent with preserved grammar and repetition but shows prominent paraphasias (semantic and phonemic), circumlocutions, jargon, and perseveration. Comprehension of simple commands is severely impaired; cannot follow two-step commands. Unable to read or write (alexia and agraphia). Pattern consistent with transcortical sensory aphasia. Attention and concentration difficult to assess due to language barrier. MMSE not obtainable due to comprehension deficits.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive 3mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Face symmetric. Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. No dysarthria appreciated despite language disturbance.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No drift on pronator testing. No fasciculations or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. No dysmetria or intention tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, narrow-based gait. Tandem walking intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No signs of meningismus. No orofacial dyskinesias or autonomic instability noted at presentation. Low mood and apathy prominent. No asterixis.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive behavioral changes, incomprehensible speech, and drowsiness over the past 10 days
patient.history_present_illness: A 61-year-old unvaccinated man with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus presents to the emergency department with progressive behavioral abnormalities and language dysfunction over the past 10 days. The illness began approximately two weeks ago with mild fever, dry cough, and general malaise. A SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR test on the second day of illness was positive, and the patient was advised to quarantine at home with supportive care. His respiratory symptoms were mild and did not require supplemental oxygen. However, on approximately the fifth day of illness, his wife noted that he began making unusual comments, speaking in an irrelevant and sometimes incomprehensible manner, and appearing increasingly withdrawn and apathetic. Over the ensuing days, his language deterioration worsened progressively. He began producing jargon-like speech with frequent word substitutions and appeared unable to understand simple questions or follow basic instructions. His wife reports that he stopped reading the newspaper and could no longer write notes, activities he previously performed daily. He also became increasingly drowsy, spending most of the day sleeping, and showing little interest in his surroundings. There were no witnessed seizures, no limb weakness, no abnormal limb movements, no gait difficulty, and no bladder or bowel incontinence. There were no headache, neck stiffness, or photophobia reported. The patient was initially evaluated at an outside hospital on day 9 of illness, where he was found to have low serum sodium and was started on correction per institutional protocol. Despite normalization of his sodium levels over several days, his behavioral and language abnormalities persisted without improvement, prompting referral to the current institution on day 15. His wife specifically denies any recent changes in medications, no new substance exposures, and no prior history of psychiatric illness. She notes he was in his usual state of health and cognitively intact prior to the onset of respiratory symptoms.

background.anterior_rhythm: Low-voltage beta activity, within normal limits
background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep spindles and vertex waves during drowsiness

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient cooperation limitations
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: This is a largely normal EEG for age with mild intermittent generalized theta slowing. No epileptiform discharges, no electrographic seizures, and no periodic patterns identified. The mild generalized slowing is non-specific and may be seen with metabolic encephalopathy, medication effect, or mild diffuse cerebral dysfunction. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Limited cooperation; hyperventilation not performed. Recording duration 30 minutes. No sleep-deprived protocol.

findings.size: Approximately 4 cm in maximum extent along the left superior temporal and inferior parietal gyri

findings.location: Left insular cortex and adjacent temporal operculum

signal_characteristics.note: Mild generalized cerebral volume loss appropriate for age

additional_observations: No abnormal leptomeningeal or parenchymal enhancement on post-gadolinium sequences
No central pontine or extrapontine myelinolysis
No periventricular white matter lesions suggestive of demyelinating disease
Paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear
mri.impression: Asymmetric left-hemispheric cortical and subcortical signal abnormality involving the temporo-parietal region, left insula, and left thalamus with cortical ribboning and DWI restriction but normal ADC values. The pattern of cortical ribboning with restricted diffusion but normal ADC is atypical for acute ischemic stroke and raises a broad differential including Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, viral encephalitis, post-ictal change, autoimmune encephalitis, or mitochondrial disease. The unilateral distribution is somewhat unusual. No evidence of osmotic demyelination. Clinical correlation is essential. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon criteria met)
Non-specific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads (V5, V6, I, aVL) — likely strain pattern associated with LVH
No ST elevation or depression suggestive of acute ischemia
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 80 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria with associated repolarization changes. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia detected.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 134 mEq/L (L), Glucose 168 mg/dL (H), CRP 1.8 mg/dL (H), ESR 22 mm/hr (H), HbA1c 7.4 % (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Sodium 134 mEq/L (low) — residual hyponatremia, recently corrected from 126
Glucose 168 mg/dL (high) — known DM2, stress hyperglycemia
CRP 1.8 mg/dL (high) — mildly elevated, likely post-infectious

csf.glucose_ratio: 0.40 (CSF glucose/serum glucose, borderline low given serum glucose 168)
csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 16 cmH2O. WBC: 3 cells/uL (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 66 mg/dL (mildly elevated). Glucose: 68 mg/dL (ratio 0.40 (CSF glucose/serum glucose, borderline low given serum glucose 168)). SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR: Negative. SARS-CoV-2 IgG (CSF): Positive (index 3.2, reference <1.0). SARS-CoV-2 Spike Protein Antibodies (CSF): Positive. HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. VZV PCR: Negative. Gram stain: No organisms seen. Bacterial culture: No growth at 48 hours. Cytology: No malignant cells. Oligoclonal bands: Absent. IgG index: 0.58 (normal <0.70).

findings.location: Left temporal lobe (superior and middle temporal gyri)
Left basifrontal cortex (orbitofrontal and inferior frontal gyri)
Right hemisphere, bilateral basal ganglia, bilateral thalami, cerebellum, brainstem

output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates a striking pattern of severe left temporal, moderate left parieto-occipital, and moderate left basifrontal hypometabolism. The right hemisphere and deep structures show normal symmetric metabolism. HSV encephalitis typically produces hypermetabolism in the acute phase, though late-stage hypometabolism is possible. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 15 cmH2O. WBC: 4 cells/uL (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 58 mg/dL (mildly elevated). Glucose: 72 mg/dL (ratio 0.55 (normal)). Repeat SARS-CoV-2 IgG (CSF): Positive (index 2.8, reference <1.0). Anti-NMDA-R antibodies (CSF, repeat): Negative. Oligoclonal bands (repeat): 1 band present (equivocal, reference: <2 bands). IgG index (repeat): 0.62 (normal <0.70). Neopterin (CSF): Elevated at 38 nmol/L (reference <20). IL-6 (CSF): Elevated at 18.5 pg/mL (reference <7).

signal_characteristics.note: Clear lung fields, no consolidation, ground glass opacity, or pleural effusion

findings.location: Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, kidneys

signal_characteristics.note: Consistent with known BPH. No suspicious lesions.

output.impression: No evidence of malignancy or lymphadenopathy on CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis. Lungs are clear without evidence of COVID pneumonia. No ovarian teratoma or other paraneoplastic lesion identified (note: male patient, ovarian teratoma not applicable). Clinical correlation recommended.

results.title: Post-COVID-19 Autoimmune Encephalitis: A Systematic Review
results.key_finding: Post-COVID autoimmune encephalitis can present with seronegative antibody panels in up to 40% of cases. Intrathecal SARS-CoV-2 antibodies with negative viral PCR suggest immune-mediated pathogenesis.

results.title: SARS-CoV-2 and autoimmune encephalitis: clinical features and outcomes
results.key_finding: Molecular mimicry between SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and neuronal surface antigens is a proposed mechanism. Most patients respond to IVIG or plasma exchange, supporting an immune-mediated etiology.

results.title: Cortical ribboning and autoimmune encephalitis in the era of COVID-19
results.key_finding: Cortical ribboning on MRI, traditionally associated with CJD, has been reported in post-COVID autoimmune encephalitis cases. FDG-PET showing cortical hypometabolism without striatal involvement helps distinguish from prion disease.

output.summary: Post-COVID autoimmune encephalitis is an increasingly recognized entity. It can present with seronegative antibody panels, cortical MRI changes mimicking CJD, and intrathecal SARS-CoV-2 antibodies without active viral replication in CSF. Key differentiating features from CJD include negative RT-QuIC, absence of rapidly progressive dementia, normal 14-3-3 protein, and clinical response to immunotherapy (IVIG, steroids, or plasma exchange). The mechanism is thought to involve molecular mimicry or bystander immune activation triggered by SARS-CoV-2.

interactions: Metformin: No direct interaction. Monitor blood glucose as IVIG preparations may contain maltose/sucrose, causing falsely elevated glucose readings on certain glucose monitors.
Aspirin: Low-dose aspirin is generally compatible. IVIG increases thrombotic risk; aspirin may be beneficial.
contraindications: IgA deficiency — risk of anaphylaxis. Check IgA levels prior to infusion.
Known hypersensitivity to human immunoglobulin preparations
warnings: Renal impairment risk: monitor renal function (BUN/creatinine) during and after infusion. Sucrose-containing preparations carry higher risk.
Thrombotic events: IVIG increases risk of DVT, PE, stroke. Ensure adequate hydration. Consider prophylactic aspirin.
Volume overload: monitor in patients with cardiac comorbidities or hypertension.
Aseptic meningitis: paradoxically may cause headache, fever, nuchal rigidity 24-48 hours post-infusion.
output.formulary_status: Available; requires neurology attending authorization for off-label autoimmune encephalitis indication
alternatives: High-dose IV methylprednisolone (1g/day for 3-5 days) as first-line alternative
Plasma exchange (PLEX) 5-7 sessions if IVIG not tolerated or insufficient response

output.query: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease versus autoimmune encephalitis differential cortical ribboning

results.title: Distinguishing CJD from autoimmune encephalitis: a clinical and radiological challenge
results.key_finding: Autoimmune encephalitis can closely mimic CJD on MRI with cortical DWI restriction. Key differentiators: CJD shows caudate/putamen involvement in 70% of cases, has positive RT-QuIC (sensitivity >92%, specificity >99%), and progresses rapidly over weeks. Autoimmune encephalitis may stabilize or improve with immunotherapy.

results.title: MRI mimics of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease: an imaging review
results.key_finding: CJD mimics on MRI include autoimmune encephalitis, post-ictal changes, mitochondrial disease, hypoglycemia, and hepatic encephalopathy. The combination of cortical ribboning with normal ADC and absence of caudate/putamen involvement should prompt consideration of non-prion etiologies.

output.summary: Differentiating CJD from autoimmune encephalitis is a well-recognized diagnostic challenge. CJD typically shows bilateral cortical and striatal (caudate/putamen) DWI restriction with reduced ADC, rapidly progressive cognitive decline over weeks, and positive RT-QuIC. Autoimmune encephalitis may show similar cortical MRI changes but often has normal ADC, unilateral predominance, absence of striatal involvement, and a subacute rather than rapidly progressive course. A negative RT-QuIC is highly reassuring against CJD.

differential.likelihood: Initially high based on MRI pattern, but excluded by negative RT-QuIC, negative 14-3-3, normal tau, and subacute rather than rapidly progressive course
differential.key_distinguishing: CJD shows positive RT-QuIC (>99% specific), rapidly progressive dementia over weeks, typically bilateral cortical and striatal involvement; all negative/absent here

differential.likelihood: Moderate based on temporal lobe involvement, but excluded by negative HSV PCR, normal CSF cell count, and clinical improvement with IVIG without acyclovir
differential.key_distinguishing: HSV encephalitis shows positive CSF HSV PCR, hemorrhagic temporal lobe changes, more acute febrile presentation with CSF pleocytosis

differential.likelihood: Low-moderate given history of hyponatremia correction, but excluded by imaging and clinical phenotype
differential.key_distinguishing: ODS involves central pons and/or basal ganglia, presents with extrapyramidal features or locked-in syndrome; absent here. Symptoms preceded hyponatremia correction.

differential.likelihood: Low — post-infectious setting supports consideration, but imaging pattern is discordant
differential.key_distinguishing: ADEM typically shows periventricular white matter lesions, multifocal demyelination; this case shows predominantly grey matter cortical pathology

differential.likelihood: Low — cortical DWI restriction can be seen post-ictally, but excluded
differential.key_distinguishing: No witnessed seizures, normal EEG without epileptiform activity rules out post-ictal etiology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging to characterize the neurological presentation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporo-parietal cortical ribboning with DWI restriction and normal ADC, left thalamic signal abnormality

optimal_actions.action: Perform EEG to evaluate for seizures and encephalopathic patterns
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal or mild non-specific slowing; absence of periodic sharp wave complexes (argues against CJD) and no epileptiform discharges (rules out post-ictal etiology)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory panel including metabolic, inflammatory, and thyroid studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia, mildly elevated CRP, normal thyroid function, normal ammonia

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including infectious and autoimmune markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Near-normal cell count, mildly elevated protein, negative HSV/VZV PCR, negative SARS-CoV-2 PCR but positive intrathecal SARS-CoV-2 antibodies

optimal_actions.action: Order prion disease biomarkers (14-3-3 protein, RT-QuIC) given MRI cortical ribboning pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative 14-3-3 protein, negative RT-QuIC — effectively excludes CJD

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel and paraneoplastic panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All known antibodies negative — supports seronegative autoimmune encephalitis diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Request FDG-PET brain to further characterize the distribution of cortical dysfunction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal, parieto-occipital, and basifrontal hypometabolism without striatal involvement — pattern more consistent with autoimmune encephalitis than CJD

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on post-COVID autoimmune encephalitis and CJD mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting post-COVID autoimmune encephalitis as diagnosis; differentiating features from CJD

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for empiric immunotherapy (IVIG)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: IVIG generally safe with current medications; monitor renal function and glucose

optimal_actions.action: Initiate empiric immunotherapy with IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days as diagnostic and therapeutic trial
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical improvement would support autoimmune etiology

critical_actions: Order CSF analysis with HSV PCR to rule out herpes encephalitis given temporal lobe involvement
Order prion disease biomarkers (RT-QuIC, 14-3-3 protein) given cortical ribboning MRI pattern mimicking CJD
Order autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel (CSF and serum)
Initiate empiric immunotherapy (IVIG or high-dose corticosteroids) once infectious etiologies are excluded
Do NOT delay immunotherapy waiting for antibody results if clinical suspicion is high and infections excluded
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antipsychotic medications without considering organic encephalitis (risk of masking symptoms and neuroleptic sensitivity)
Rapid correction of hyponatremia beyond recommended rate (risk of osmotic demyelination — already addressed at outside hospital)
Administering acyclovir empirically without considering the broader differential (while empiric acyclovir is reasonable pending HSV PCR, it should not delay immunotherapy workup)
Performing brain biopsy as initial diagnostic step (invasive and unnecessary given available non-invasive workup)
Dismissing presentation as psychiatric without organic workup given clear MRI abnormalities
key_reasoning_points: Cortical ribboning with DWI restriction but normal ADC on MRI mimics CJD, but negative RT-QuIC and 14-3-3 effectively rule out prion disease
Temporal lobe involvement raises concern for HSV encephalitis, but negative HSV PCR, near-normal CSF cell count, and improvement with IVIG without acyclovir argue against it

History of hyponatremia correction raises concern for osmotic demyelination, but absence of pontine/basal ganglia lesions, presence of language dysfunction (not extrapyramidal features), and symptom onset before sodium correction argue against ODS
ADEM is excluded by the predominantly grey matter cortical distribution rather than periventricular white matter involvement
Normal EEG rules out post-ictal change as the cause of cortical DWI restriction
Intrathecal SARS-CoV-2 antibodies with negative CSF viral PCR indicate immune-mediated process rather than direct CNS viral invasion
Negative complete autoimmune and paraneoplastic panels indicate seronegative autoimmune encephalitis — a recognized entity especially in post-COVID cases
The temporal relationship between SARS-CoV-2 infection and neurological symptom onset supports post-infectious autoimmune pathogenesis
FDG-PET showing unilateral cortical hypometabolism without striatal involvement further distinguishes from CJD
Clinical response to IVIG (empiric immunotherapy) serves as both therapeutic intervention and diagnostic confirmation

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: MRI cortical ribboning pattern strongly mimics CJD, temporal lobe involvement raises concern for HSV encephalitis, recent hyponatremia correction suggests osmotic demyelination, all standard autoimmune antibody panels are negative (seronegative), and the key diagnostic clue — intrathecal SARS-CoV-2 antibodies with elevated CSF cytokines — requires specific follow-up testing and clinical reasoning to integrate. The atypical demographics (61-year-old male, whereas NMDAR encephalitis classically affects young women) and post-COVID context add further complexity.
